items.q: What does karma mean in Hindu and Buddhist thought?
items.correct: The principle that actions have moral consequences shaping future experience or rebirth — not fate, but ethical cause and effect built into reality.
items.misconception: Karma is when bad things happen to bad people as punishment.
items.random_wrong: Karma is a god who decides what happens to you.
items.why: Students treat karma as cosmic justice meted out by a deity. In its philosophical origins it is impersonal moral causation — your own actions, not external judgement, shape your circumstances.

items.q: What does utilitarianism say is right action?
items.correct: The action that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number — judged by consequences, not intentions or rules.
items.misconception: Whatever makes you personally happy is the right thing to do.
items.random_wrong: Utilitarianism says you should always tell the truth.
items.why: Students reduce utilitarianism to egoism. It is impartial — your own happiness counts no more than anyone else's. Personal happiness alone misses the aggregative core.

items.q: Who do Sikhs consider their living guru today?
items.correct: The Guru Granth Sahib — the Sikh scripture, declared the eternal guru by Guru Gobind Singh after the line of ten human gurus ended.
items.misconception: Sikhs follow a current living guru as their leader.
items.random_wrong: Sikhs consider the Dalai Lama their current guru.
items.why: The line of human gurus ended in 1708; guruship passed to scripture. Students assume a continuing line of personal teachers — missing the distinctive Sikh teaching about the eternal guru in the text.
Correct answer: Bodh Gaya. “Sarnath” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What was Siddhartha's age, traditionally, at renunciation?
items.why: Correct answer: 29. “12” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate traditional year of the Buddha's parinirvana (BCE)?
items.why: The real figure is 483. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
